includedSources.name: The Dark Side of the Boom

skippedSources.name: McKinsey & Company
skippedSources.reason: Link-roundup teaser (one descriptive sentence on an AP CEO interview) — no original analysis or data in the email body itself.

items.title: Xi and Trump set for round two — AI is now the agenda
items.idea: The US and China have confirmed AI talks ahead of a second Xi-Trump White House meeting on September 24. The fight is explicitly framed as open-vs-closed models and "distillation" charges, with Microsoft, Meta and Amazon now lobbying to use Chinese open models rather than defend the closed-model moat their own labs are building.
items.angle: The closed-model lobby (OpenAI/Anthropic) is losing the argument inside its own government's biggest tech companies — that's a tell for where model economics go next, well before September.
items.tweetA: Microsoft, Meta and Amazon are now the ones telling the White House to embrace Chinese open models. When Big Tech lobbies against its own AI labs' moat, that's not policy talk — that's a pricing signal for every closed-model subscription business.
items.tweetB: Sept 24 is the date to circle: Xi-Trump round two, with "AI distillation" as the central charge. Whoever wins the open-vs-closed framing before then sets the ceiling on every AI subscription multiple in the market.

items.title: China's frontier models are now measured in trillions, not billions — and they're free
items.idea: Moonshot's Kimi K3 (2.8 trillion parameters) and Alibaba's Qwen 3.7 Max (2.4 trillion parameters) both launched around China's Shanghai AI conference, going toe-to-toe with US frontier models — and both are being given away for free.
items.angle: This is the same wall Deutsche Bank hit in its own note this window (see the "format war" item below) — two independent sources converging on the same free-model threat to $700B of 2026 US AI capex.
items.tweetA: Kimi K3 at 2.8 trillion parameters, Qwen 3.7 Max at 2.4T — both free, both from China, both dropped right before the Xi-Trump AI summit. The "moat" argument for closed US labs gets harder to make with a straight face every month.
items.tweetB: Everyone's debating whether China can compete on chips. Nobody's pricing in that they've stopped competing on chips and started giving away the software layer for free. That's the actual threat to $700B of 2026 US AI capex.

items.title: Bessent wants to sanction China for "stealing" AI — Nvidia's own bet allegedly did the same thing
items.idea: Treasury's Bessent is floating sanctions on China over AI-model "theft"/distillation, even as Thinking Machines — the newest US open-weights entrant, backed by Nvidia — is reported to have distilled its own model using Chinese AI outputs.
items.angle: The sanctions threat is a September negotiating chip, not an enforceable policy, because the practice runs in both directions inside the US's own ecosystem.
items.tweetA: Bessent floats sanctioning China for "AI theft" via distillation. Meanwhile Nvidia's own portfolio bet, Thinking Machines, is reported to have distilled its model off Chinese AI. You can't sanction a technique your own ecosystem depends on.
items.tweetB: The distillation fight isn't US vs China. It's every lab vs every other lab. Watch what survives contact with Microsoft, Meta and Amazon's China lobbying before you take the sanctions talk at face value.

items.title: Energy just won the year, and 19% of its stocks are at 52-week highs
items.idea: Energy is the S&P's top sector YTD (+28.9%), leading 121 of 136 trading days — with 19% of its 21 constituents at 52-week highs versus 5.9% for the next-best sector (Consumer Staples). If it holds, 2026 is only the third time since 2021 Energy finishes #1.
items.angle: This is happening while oil is spiking on Hormuz/Red Sea escalation (see the geopolitics items below) — the sector move isn't sentiment, it's a direct read-through from the same conflict driving crude past $90.
items.tweetA: 19% of Energy stocks are at 52-week highs. Second place isn't close — 5.9%. A sector leading the S&P this hard, this concentrated, while Hormuz shipping seizes up, is not a coincidence trade. $XLE
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching the AI trade for leadership. Energy has quietly led 121 of 136 trading days this year and nobody's talking about it because it's not sexy. The most obvious trade is always the one nobody tweets about.

charts.desc: Sector-leadership chart plotting Energy/Materials/Industrials/Tech YTD dominance by trading day — tells the whole story at a glance.

items.title: The investor behind Aurion's $481M sale to Agnico Eagle breaks down 14 years in gold-copper
items.idea: David Lotan, Aurion Resources' largest shareholder and former non-executive chairman, discusses the 14-year path to Agnico Eagle's $481M takeover, plus a separate large US copper M&A deal in Arizona and his views on the current geopolitical and AI backdrop.
items.angle: Gold-copper M&A is accelerating as majors backfill reserves — a signal for junior miner re-rating potential across the sector, not just this one deal.
items.tweetA: A $481M exit after 14 years holding a junior gold explorer. Agnico Eagle didn't overpay for Aurion — they paid for optionality majors can no longer generate organically. Watch which junior copper names get the same treatment next. $AEM
items.tweetB: There's a US copper M&A deal happening in Arizona alongside the Aurion gold deal, in the same conversation. Copper and gold M&A moving together isn't coincidence — it's majors racing to lock in reserves before the critical-minerals repricing gets more expensive.

items.title: Turbopuffer's founder did "napkin math" and found vector search was priced for suckers
items.idea: Simon Eskildsen used first-principles cost calculations to discover vector-search infrastructure was priced far above the theoretical compute limits — turbopuffer was born from that gap, and Cursor became its first customer after turbopuffer raised just $8M in seed funding.
items.angle: The "napkin math" lesson generalizes to every AI infra category right now — most vendors are pricing off legacy assumptions, not physics.
items.tweetA: Eskildsen's line that stuck with me: too much VC funding is about stroking founder ego, not solving a real cost problem. Turbopuffer exists because someone did the arithmetic on vector-search pricing instead of trusting the market rate.
items.tweetB: $8M seed round, and Cursor became customer number one. In this AI infra cycle, the smallest checks are going to founders who can do napkin math faster than they can pitch a deck — the opposite of the mega-round era.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Correlation is at record lows — and that's exactly what should worry you
items.idea: Bloomberg's Simon White notes US large-cap stock correlation has fallen to record lows, keeping the VIX pinned down even as hyperscalers pile on debt to fund the AI capex boom — the calm is masking, not removing, a growing single-factor leverage risk.
items.angle: Low VIX plus record AI-capex debt issuance is the classic setup where the "quiet" market is the most dangerous one.
items.tweetA: Record-low correlation is being read as a "stock-pickers market." Simon White's actual point: it's scattering a single, growing risk — AI capex debt — across a thousand tickers so the VIX can't see it. The floorboards are creaking under a very quiet room.
items.tweetB: The VIX says nothing's wrong. The correlation matrix says something's being hidden. When hyperscaler debt issuance rises and index volatility doesn't follow, that gap is the trade, not the confirmation.

items.title: Polymarket says 56% odds BTC hits $67,500 this month
items.idea: Polymarket pricing shows a 56% probability of Bitcoin reaching $67,500 in July — a number the newsletter author calls conservative — set against hedge funds simultaneously dumping US tech stocks and shorting Nvidia at scale.
items.angle: Crypto positioning is turning bullish while equity positioning in AI-adjacent names is turning bearish — a rotation, not a simple risk-on/risk-off signal.
items.tweetA: Polymarket: 56% odds BTC prints $67,500 this month. Same week, hedge funds are dumping US tech at a record pace and piling into NVDA shorts. That's not risk-off — that's money rotating out of AI equity beta and into crypto beta.
items.tweetB: Prediction markets are pricing BTC upside more confidently than equity desks are pricing NVDA downside conviction. When the crowd trusts a meme market more than its own short thesis, fade the short thesis first.

items.title: Hedge funds are dumping US tech at a record pace — and shorting NVIDIA hard
items.idea: Per the newsletter's linked chart, hedge funds are reducing US tech exposure at a record rate while simultaneously building large short positions in Nvidia, even as Jamie Dimon publicly warns "do not buy stocks right now."
items.angle: This is the first hard positioning data in this window showing real distribution in the AI trade, distinct from softer sentiment pieces elsewhere in the mail.
items.tweetA: Forget the sentiment surveys — the actual hedge fund flow data this week shows record-pace tech selling plus fresh NVDA shorts. That's not caution, that's distribution. Sentiment lags positioning every time.
items.tweetB: Dimon says don't buy stocks. The funds already didn't — they're the ones selling into this rally, not the ones cheering it. When the smart money's flow and the loud money's warning line up, that's usually the tell you act on.

charts.desc: Chart of hedge fund tech-selling pace and NVDA short build — the kind of chart that tells the story on its own.

items.title: Ondo just took 79% of all tokenized-stock trading volume
items.idea: Ondo captured 79% of onchain tokenized-stock volume this week at $361.1M — a dominant, lopsided share this early in the tokenized-equities category.
items.angle: Market share this concentrated this early usually means the category is about to get a lot bigger, not that it's a fad — worth tracking Ondo against Robinhood Chain's competing distribution push below.
items.tweetA: Ondo: 79% share of tokenized-stock trading volume. $361M weekly volume sounds small until you realize it's concentrated in one protocol. Market share this lopsided this early usually means the category is about to get a lot bigger.
items.tweetB: Tokenized equities are consolidating into a duopoly before most brokerages have even shipped a competing product. Ondo's 79% share is the number to watch, not the $361M headline.

items.title: Robinhood Chain out-added every other blockchain combined on stock-token holders
items.idea: Robinhood Chain gained 240,000 more stock-token holders than any other blockchain over the past 30 days — a distribution edge coming from Robinhood's existing retail user base, not crypto-native marketing.
items.angle: Distribution beats decentralization ideology every time tokenized equities go mainstream — Robinhood's retail funnel did in a month what pure-crypto rails spent years failing to do.
items.tweetA: Robinhood Chain added 240K stock-token holders in 30 days — more than every other chain combined. This is what happens when a tokenization play gets bolted onto an existing brokerage funnel instead of bootstrapping distribution from zero.
items.tweetB: Forget "decentralization" as the pitch for tokenized equities. Robinhood just won the category on distribution alone — 240K new holders in a month is a customer-acquisition story, not a blockchain story.

items.title: Dollar stablecoin supply just shrank $4.7B in 30 days
items.idea: USD-denominated stablecoin supply is down $4.7 billion over the past 30 days — a contraction signal even as onchain trading volumes keep climbing (Uniswap did $15B+ in protocol volume this period).
items.angle: Falling stablecoin float alongside rising DEX volume suggests capital velocity is up even as total dollars parked onchain shrinks — a turnover story, not a demand story.
items.tweetA: $4.7B of USD stablecoin supply vanished in a month while Uniswap did $15B+ in volume. That combination means the same dollars are turning over faster, not that fewer dollars are showing up.
items.tweetB: Everyone tracks stablecoin supply as a crypto demand proxy. It just fell $4.7B in 30 days during one of the more active onchain volume months this year. Supply and activity decoupling is the actual story, and nobody's asking why.

items.title: Hyperliquid is taking share from centralized exchanges at a rapid clip
items.idea: Onchain data shows Hyperliquid rapidly stealing trading share from centralized exchanges, continuing a trend that's been building all year as perp trading migrates onchain.
items.angle: The CEX-to-onchain perp migration everyone predicted in 2023 is showing up in the data now — worth tracking against any CEX-token or CEX-equity exposure.
items.tweetA: Hyperliquid isn't just growing, it's taking share directly from centralized exchanges. Every basis point of that migration is a basis point of CEX perp-fee revenue that isn't coming back.
items.tweetB: The CEX-to-onchain perp migration everyone predicted in 2023 is showing up in the data now, not later. Hyperliquid's share gains are the clearest single chart for that thesis this year.

items.title: London Stock Exchange is going 24/7 to fight crypto on its own turf
items.idea: The London Stock Exchange announced round-the-clock trading specifically to compete with 24/7 crypto platforms — the first major legacy exchange to concede that continuous markets are now table stakes.
items.angle: This is the clearest evidence yet that crypto market structure (always-on trading) has become the standard traditional finance has to match, not the other way around.
items.tweetA: LSE going 24/7 to compete with crypto isn't a crypto story, it's a market-structure surrender. The 300-year-old exchange just admitted continuous trading is the new baseline, not a novelty Robinhood invented.
items.tweetB: When the London Stock Exchange redesigns its market hours around what crypto built by default, that's the tell that "crypto market structure" has quietly become just "market structure."

items.title: SPY reclaims every moving average right before the week that decides the AI trade
items.idea: SPY closed under its 50-day for a second session, looked "bleak," then gapped back above all key moving averages the next morning — with Texas Instruments and Google reporting Tuesday and Intel Thursday set to set the tone for the AI trade this week.
items.angle: The market punished anyone who de-risked into weakness one day before the actual earnings catalysts even arrived.
items.tweetA: Second close under the 50-day looked like the start of something. Instead SPY gapped back over every moving average the next morning — right before TXN, GOOGL and INTC report. This market keeps punishing people for managing risk one day too early.
items.tweetB: The real test isn't today's gap-up, it's Thursday. Intel's guide after TXN and Google report will tell you whether this is a real AI-trade reset or just another head-fake before the next leg.

items.title: BlackRock's Aladdin is quietly a $2B software business inside a $15T asset manager
items.idea: BlackRock's Aladdin technology-services business generates roughly $2B in revenue — nearly matching Cloudflare — across 130,000+ users, while its Asimov system now uses AI agents to scan research, filings and emails for portfolio-relevant signals, with a new no-code agent builder (RockAI) rolling out to employees.
items.angle: The largest asset manager in the world just quietly became a software company with agent infrastructure most fintech startups can't match in scale or auditability.
items.tweetA: BlackRock's Aladdin does about as much revenue as Cloudflare. Except Aladdin's customers are the world's largest institutions, and it just added AI agents that scan filings and emails for portfolio risk. "BlackRock is a tech company" has a number now.
items.tweetB: Asimov isn't a chatbot bolted onto Aladdin — it's agents reading filings and emails across 130,000 users with a full audit trail. That's the AI deployment blueprint every regulated industry copies, not the consumer-app version everyone benchmarks against.

items.title: AI agents are about to become crypto's biggest investor class — and most tokens can't answer "what do I own?"
items.idea: The newsletter argues AI agents will soon be the largest class of crypto investors, deploying capital in seconds, yet most tokens can't answer basic ownership questions; the CLARITY Act cleared the Senate Banking Committee 15-9 in May 2026 but still needs full Senate passage and reconciliation with the House version.
items.angle: Machine investors won't tolerate the ambiguity human retail did — token structures built on vague governance promises are about to face their first genuinely rational buyer.
items.tweetA: An AI agent can open a wallet and deploy capital in seconds, but it can't answer "what do I actually own" for most tokens — because the answer is scattered across a DAO, a foundation and a dev company. Agents won't tolerate that ambiguity the way retail did.
items.tweetB: CLARITY Act cleared Senate Banking 15-9 in May. It classifies tokens as commodities vs securities — it still doesn't tell an AI agent what claim it has on revenue or IP. Regulatory clarity and ownership clarity are two different problems, and only one is being solved.

items.title: Applied Intuition beat Nvidia and Ansys in a GM bidding war — then got put on Navy warships
items.idea: Applied Intuition, now valued at $15B, won a GM tooling RFP against 28 bidders including Nvidia and Ansys, now counts 18 of the top 20 non-Chinese automakers as customers, and has deployments on US Navy warships and with the Army, plus driverless Level-4 trucks running in Japan.
items.angle: The "picks and shovels" thesis for physical AI just produced a bigger winner than most of the vertically-integrated robotaxi bets it was founded to avoid competing with.
items.tweetA: Applied Intuition beat NVIDIA in a bidding war for a tooling contract. Let that sink in. The "boring" infrastructure layer for autonomy just outcompeted the chip giant on its own turf, and it's now running on Navy warships.
items.tweetB: Cruise doesn't exist anymore. Applied Intuition, the tooling company Cruise didn't think it needed, is now worth $15B and shipping to 18 of the top 20 automakers. Betting on the arms dealer over the army was the correct trade in autonomy.

items.title: NVIDIA's new CPU beat AMD's 256-core chip using 80 fewer cores
items.idea: NVIDIA's Vera CPU (176 cores) scored 925 on SPECrate integer throughput against AMD's EPYC Turin (256 cores) at 898 — 3% more performance from 31% fewer cores — with memory bandwidth staying flat past 1TB/s vs Turin's cliff at 350-400GB/s. NVIDIA compared different memory generations (LPDDR5X-9600 vs DDR5-6400) though, leaving AMD an obvious rebuttal at its July 22-23 event.
items.angle: The real argument isn't core count, it's cost-per-completed-agent-task — NVIDIA just weaponized a benchmark to justify owning the CPU socket, not just the GPU.
items.tweetA: 176 NVIDIA cores beat 256 AMD cores by 3% on throughput. But NVIDIA ran its own chip on faster memory than AMD's — convenient. AMD gets its rebuttal tomorrow at its own event; watch whether a same-memory number closes this gap entirely. $NVDA $AMD
items.tweetB: The real metric here isn't tokens per second, it's cost per completed agent task — and that's a number NVIDIA controls by owning the CPU next to the GPU. If Vera works as advertised, NVIDIA just built a second moat nobody was pricing in.

charts.desc: SPECrate benchmark tables and a memory-bandwidth-vs-latency curve embedded in the piece — the bandwidth cliff chart alone is tweet-worthy.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Saudi tankers just reversed course in the Red Sea — the second chokepoint just closed
items.idea: Tankers carrying Saudi crude reportedly U-turned in the Red Sea after Houthi warnings, while Hormuz traffic sits near a standstill and Gulf infrastructure (power, desalination, air defense) in Bahrain and Kuwait is being hit directly — the market is now pricing two simultaneous chokepoints, not one.
items.angle: This compounds directly with Neil Sethi's independent report on the same conflict below — the tanker U-turns are the concrete, trackable evidence behind the oil price move everyone's reading as "just headlines."
items.tweetA: Two Saudi tankers reversed course in the Red Sea today. Combine that with Hormuz traffic near zero and strikes hitting Kuwaiti desalination plants, and this stops being a "geopolitical premium" story — it's a physical supply-routing failure. $USO $XLE
items.tweetB: The market keeps asking if oil holds above $90. Wrong question. The right one: what happens to inflation and the Fed if BOTH Hormuz and the Red Sea stay constrained through August? That's a $110 scenario nobody's stress-testing yet.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Deutsche Bank calls open-vs-closed AI the next format war — and $700B of capex is the stake
items.idea: DB strategist Adrian Cox frames the open-vs-proprietary AI contest as a defining format war, arguing another Chinese open-weight release has revived DeepSeek-shock-style fears just as Google, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta and Oracle plan roughly $700B of 2026 AI capex — up about 70% year over year.
items.angle: This is the sell-side confirmation of the same story in Michael Parekh's note above (Kimi K3/Qwen 3.7 Max) — independent sources converging on the same format-war thesis the same afternoon.
items.tweetA: $700B of hyperscaler capex in 2026, up 70% year over year, all built on the assumption that proprietary models keep pricing power. Deutsche Bank is now on record calling that assumption a "format war" the labs might lose. That's the bear case in one sentence.
items.tweetB: Everyone models AI capex as a demand story. Deutsche Bank just modeled it as a moat story — and moats are exactly what commodity-priced Chinese open models are built to destroy. Watch hyperscaler capex guides next earnings season for the tell.

items.title: The world's arable land is shrinking exactly as AI starts optimizing every acre of what's left
items.idea: India's arable land fell from 385.6M to 381.6M acres (2019-2021) and China's from 295M to 269M, even as global population keeps rising — precision farming (AI, sensors, robotics) is being deployed specifically to squeeze more yield per shrinking acre, which will eventually collide with how "organic" certification is legally defined.
items.angle: A quiet, multi-decade supply constraint that AI is racing to offset — a genuine agri-tech/commodities thesis hiding inside a farmers-market joke article.
items.tweetA: China's arable land fell from 295M to 269M acres in two years. India's shrank too. Population isn't shrinking. That gap is exactly what AI-driven precision farming is being built to close — a real supply story wearing a lifestyle-blog costume.
items.tweetB: USDA organic rules were written for a world without per-plant AI optimization. When precision farming can dose water and nitrogen down to the individual plant, "organic" becomes a legal category fighting a technological one. That fight hasn't started — but the land math forces it.

items.title: A DeepMind researcher quit rather than stay silent on the Pentagon deal Google promised never to sign
items.idea: Google DeepMind's 2018 pledge against lethal autonomous weapons was quietly reversed in a February 2025 principles update; a researcher who tried to stop the resulting unrestricted Pentagon deal resigned after Jeff Dean criticized it publicly but didn't threaten to leave, while Anthropic — which refused a similar deal — was labeled a "supply chain risk" by the government in apparent retaliation.
items.angle: A testable case of whether "responsible AI" pledges survive contact with a defense contract, with real read-through for how much credibility labs' safety commitments deserve going forward.
items.tweetA: Google pledged in 2018 to never build lethal autonomous weapons. It quietly rewrote that pledge in February 2025, then signed an unrestricted Pentagon deal. Anthropic refused a similar deal and got labeled a "supply chain risk" by the same government. Pick which one you'd trust with an AI safety commitment.
items.tweetB: Every AI safety pledge is a promise until it costs something. Google's just proved its 2018 pledge was worth exactly one researcher's resignation and zero binding concessions. Price every future "responsible AI" announcement from any lab accordingly.

items.title: Memory and AI stocks are gapping — but nothing's trending clean enough to chase
items.idea: Pre-market futures show SPX +0.38%/Nasdaq +1.32% led by memory and AI names; Micron is testing resistance at 937 right at its 50-day SMA while LITE gaps above its 21-day MA — the trader flags mean-reversion setups only, with no clean trend worth building new positions around.
items.angle: A useful counter-signal to the bullish "everything's ripping" narrative elsewhere in today's mail — actual traders are seeing chop, not conviction, inside the memory/AI cohort specifically.
items.tweetA: MU testing 937 right at its 50-day, LITE gapping above its 21-day — sounds bullish until you notice the trader flagging this says nothing's actually trending clean enough to add new size. Chop dressed up as momentum is still chop.
items.tweetB: The AI/memory cohort is up in the pre-market again today. The people actually trading it are describing mean-reversion setups, not breakouts. When the tape looks strong but the practitioners sound hesitant, believe the practitioners.

items.title: The UK just got a new Prime Minister overnight — and gilt yields noticed
items.idea: Andy Burnham became UK Prime Minister (replacing Starmer) with a sweeping cabinet reshuffle — Rachel Reeves out as Chancellor, replaced by John Healey — while 10-year gilt yields touched a two-month high of 5.05% before easing on weak UK wage data; Burnham has pledged to push borrowing "closer to the limits" of fiscal rules.
items.angle: A UK leadership change with an explicitly more fiscally-loose Chancellor is a gilt/GBP story the US market hasn't priced at all yet.
items.tweetA: Andy Burnham is UK Prime Minister as of Monday. Rachel Reeves is out. The new Chancellor's mandate is explicitly to push borrowing "closer to the limits" of the fiscal rules. Gilt yields already hit a two-month high — this is a GBP and UK duration story nobody in the US is watching yet.
items.tweetB: Eleven cabinet members gone overnight, a new PM promising the "biggest changes in 40 years," and a Chancellor mandated to loosen fiscal policy. That's not a headline risk event — that's a regime change in how the UK prices its own debt.

items.title: US-Iran airstrikes hit day ten as UK navy reports hits near Hormuz
items.idea: The US and Iran exchanged airstrikes for a tenth consecutive day; US Central Command targeted Iranian command centers and air defenses overnight while Iran struck Kuwait and Jordan, hitting power and desalination plants in Kuwait; the UK navy separately reported strikes on two vessels near the Strait of Hormuz.
items.angle: A second, independent data point beyond the Dark Side of the Boom oil piece above, confirming the conflict is expanding into Gulf civilian infrastructure — and adding a UK-navy vessel-strike detail neither other source mentioned.
items.tweetA: Day ten of US-Iran airstrikes, and now the UK navy is reporting strikes on vessels right at Hormuz. This isn't background noise anymore — it's a live shipping-lane conflict with a NATO navy now reporting incidents directly.
items.tweetB: Kuwait's desalination plants got hit. That's not an oil story, that's a fresh-water story for a Gulf state. When infrastructure damage moves from "energy exports" to "can people drink water," the escalation ladder just changed floors.

items.title: 87% of S&P 500 reporters have beaten estimates so far — but ADP just confirmed the job market is still cooling
items.idea: Of the roughly 54 S&P 500 companies that have reported this earnings season, 87% have topped bottom-line estimates per FactSet, even as the ADP weekly report showed continued slowing in job growth after a May peak.
items.angle: Strong beat rates this early in a season are common (low bar), but pairing it with a confirmed labor cooldown is the setup that usually keeps the Fed on a cutting bias regardless of the earnings tape.
items.tweetA: 87% beat rate on 54 reporters sounds great until you remember low bars get cleared 80%+ of the time most quarters. The actual signal today is ADP confirming the labor cooldown continued past its May peak — that's the number that moves the Fed, not the beat rate.
items.tweetB: Strong earnings, cooling jobs data, oil spiking on Hormuz — that combination is "stagflation-adjacent," not "goldilocks." The market is currently trading the earnings headline and ignoring the other two.

items.title: Fixed-income funds think the Fed hikes next week — Barry Knapp says remember February 1994
items.idea: Barry Knapp notes fixed-income hedge funds are pricing a surprise Fed hike next week; he draws a direct parallel to Greenspan's February 1994 surprise hike, which caused only an 8% equity pullback but "absolute carnage" in Treasuries, mortgages and fixed-income derivatives — and argues a Warsh-driven credibility hike now would land worse, since unlike 1994 this isn't the start of an expansion.
items.angle: A genuinely differentiated, non-consensus risk not priced anywhere else in today's mail — a specific historical parallel with a specific mechanism (credit/derivatives dislocation, not equity drawdown) for where the pain shows up first.
items.tweetA: Barry Knapp's read: if Warsh hikes for "credibility" next week, the equity drawdown will look like nothing — 1994 only cost the S&P 8%. The carnage lands in Treasuries, mortgages and fixed-income derivatives, and this time there's no expansion boom underneath to cushion it.
items.tweetB: 1994's hike surprise happened at the start of a tech-led boom. A 2026 version happens with no expansion tailwind. Same policy mistake, worse timing — Knapp's math says the credit-market fallout could exceed '94, '04 and the '13 Taper Tantrum combined.
